results.ruleId: 7 (Invisible invariants) + 5 (Coincidental correctness), Qdrant addendum (non-atomic upsert / orphaned vectors)

message.text: Systemic index outage completes every case as a "warning", never an alarm — The team flips `LEX_KRATOS_ENABLE_QDRANT=true` in a real deployment (the whole point of PR #17). Qdrant then goes unreachable for an hour (restart, network, disk-full). Every `run_full_mock` call still runs Intake→Security→…→FIRAC→Validation and returns `status="warning"` — the *same* status a single low-quality extraction produces — so the dashboards look normal and nobody pages. Meanwhile the invariant a future consumer assumes — **"a pipeline run that completed ⇒ its chunks are in the vector index"** — is silently false for every case processed during the outage. The already-planned `HybridRetrievalAgent` (`registry.py:76-84`, status `planned`) is built later on exactly that assumption; it retrieves against an index missing an hour of cases and returns confidently wrong precedent context, with no error anywhere. The best-effort decision (M1) is correct for *this* case's analysis, but it converted a loud `failed` into an indistinguishable `warning`, erasing the only signal that would have surfaced a systemic backlog.

results.ruleId: 7 (Invisible invariants) — a cross-module invariant held by convention

message.text: `case_id` min_length=1 is kept off the primary path only by the Hermes tool schema — A later edit relaxes the `lex_intake`/`lex_run_pipeline` tool schema — makes `case_id` optional, or adds a "quick triage" tool that auto-generates an ID — reasonably, since the plugin already defaults it. Now a Telegram tool call without `case_id` reaches `_case_payload`, sends `case_id=""`, and the API 422s. `_call` catches it and returns `{"error": "lexintel API retornou HTTP 422..."}` to the user, who did nothing wrong. The break is in `schemas.py` (schema) but surfaces in `case.py` (validation) via a plugin default nobody re-examined. It fails loud and is recoverable, hence low — but the two sides can drift silently until someone touches the schema.

The per-area eval gate fails *open*; its sibling gates fail *loud* — A future change constructs a thresholds dict directly instead of merging onto `DEFAULT_THRESHOLDS` (e.g. a new eval harness, a CI matrix, a test that passes a hand-built dict — `test_eval_gate_enforces_per_area_floor` already passes `DEFAULT_THRESHOLDS` explicitly, normalising the pattern). The per-area floor silently disappears; the eval goes back to global-average-only and can again green a build where one legal area is broken — the exact regression M3 was added to prevent — with no error to reveal the gate was dropped. The `run()` merge path (`run_eval.py:430-432`) protects the CLI today; a caller that bypasses it is unprotected and unwarned.

`"failed"` is now an orphan status in the orchestrator — A developer adds a new agent (or a future non-mock step) that returns `status="failed"` for a hard error, reasonably expecting — from the surviving `_response_status` branch and the `Literal` — that the framework treats `failed` as a real terminal state. It does not: the per-step guards ignore `failed`, so the pipeline runs on through every downstream legal phase, and only the final summary label reads `"failed"`. The status vocabulary advertises a capability (a distinct terminal failure) the control flow no longer backs. Low, because it needs a new producer to bite, and it surfaces in the trace rather than corrupting data.
